{

The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and data governance model.",

      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete audit trail of a delivery lifecycle: creation, pickup, transit events, proof of delivery, exception handling, and completion. Serves as the legal evidence that a shipment was fulfilled." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "The subset of a delivery record that legally proves a consignment was delivered to the correct recipient — includes signature, photo, timestamp, GPS coordinates. Required for dispute resolution and tax/customs evidence." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The rules governing how long specific categories of data are stored, driven by legal requirements, contractual obligations, and business needs — not by storage cost targets." },

      { "term": "Right of Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)", "definition": "A data subject's right to request deletion of personal data, which does NOT apply when retention is necessary for legal compliance, exercise of legal claims, or contractual obligations." },
      { "term": "Statute of Limitations", "definition": "The legal time window during which parties can bring claims. For contract disputes in most EU jurisdictions this is 3-6 years; in the UK it is 6 years. Delivery records are primary evidence in such disputes." }

      "invariant_justification": "Tax authorities can audit companies for up to 6 years (UK HMRC) or longer in fraud cases.
